ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific entry for Japanese beef curry rice combo. FOODON_00002809 (curry dish) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Japanese beef curry rice combo. A0CKW (curry, meat-based) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for Japanese beef curry rice combo. SNOMED CT is not used for this record.

serving.description: 1 combo set (curry rice with beef, half-boiled egg, and miso soup)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 550 g, comprising a bowl of Japanese curry rice with beef (approximately 350 g), one half-boiled egg (approximately 50 g), and a bowl of miso soup (approximately 150 g).

nutrient_source.title: Curry, Japanese style, with beef and rice — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables for Japanese curry rice
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a complete Japanese beef curry rice combo with egg and miso soup as served in Singapore fast-casual restaurants. Values are estimated by component-weighted averaging: Japanese curry rice with beef (USDA SR Legacy: beef stew/curry components, cooked white rice), half-boiled egg (USDA SR Legacy: egg, whole, cooked), and miso soup (FAO/INFOODS SEA). Per-100g values are derived from the estimated 550 g composite serving. Values are consistent with published ranges for Japanese-style curry rice dishes in peer-reviewed food composition literature.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice and curry-based mixed dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Japanese beef curry rice combo as a composite dish. GI of 60 is estimated based on the dominant glycemic driver being Japanese short-grain white rice (GI approximately 64–72, University of Sydney GI Database) moderated by the presence of beef, curry sauce (fat and protein content), and egg, which slow gastric emptying and reduce the effective GI of the meal. A conservative blended GI of 60 (medium category) is applied, consistent with published mixed-meal GI literature for rice-based curry dishes. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (60 × 96.25) / 100 ≈ 57.8, rounded to 58.1 using 17.5 g carbohydrate per 100g × 550g serving / 100 = 96.25g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from curry roux, miso soup, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium miso soup, as a single combo serving provides an estimated 1,705 mg sodium (approximately 74% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,300 mg), primarily from the curry roux and miso soup components.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index driven by white rice as the primary carbohydrate source
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that this combo provides approximately 96 g of available carbohydrate per serving with a medium GI of 60, resulting in a high glycemic load; portion control and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables or reducing rice quantity is recommended to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and curry roux, and high sodium from miso soup and curry sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should note that this dish contains moderate saturated fat (approximately 8.8 g per serving) and high sodium (approximately 1,705 mg per serving); choosing leaner beef cuts, reduced-sodium miso, and limiting frequency of consumption is advisable to support cardiovascular health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Curry Rice Combo is a Japanese-style fast-casual meal consisting of steamed short-grain white rice topped with a rich, mildly spiced Japanese curry sauce containing tender simmered beef pieces, served alongside a half-boiled egg and a bowl of miso soup. Japanese curry (karē raisu) is characteristically milder, sweeter, and thicker than South Asian curries, typically made from a roux-based curry block containing wheat flour, vegetable oil, curry spices, and often apple or honey for sweetness. The beef is usually thinly sliced or cubed and slow-simmered in the curry sauce until tender. The half-boiled egg adds protein and richness, while the miso soup provides umami depth and warmth. This combo format is popularised in Singapore by Japanese fast-food chains such as Sukiya and similar gyudon-style restaurants. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense (approximately 726 kcal per serving), high in carbohydrates from white rice, moderate in protein from beef and egg, and notably high in sodium due to the curry roux and miso soup. The medium glycemic index (approximately 60) reflects the moderating effect of fat and protein on rice-driven glucose response. Clinically, the dish warrants attention for sodium content in hypertensive individuals and carbohydrate load in those managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular risk.

What is in a beef curry rice combo?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish as served in Singapore. Nutrient values are estimated by component-weighted averaging using USDA SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for the constituent components (Japanese curry rice with beef, half-boiled egg, miso soup). GI is an analogous estimate based on the dominant carbohydrate source (white rice) moderated by the mixed-meal effect. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved databases and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not consistently reported across the component sources used for this estimated composite dish. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source for this dish type. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy beef and curry roux component data. The serving size of 550 g is an estimate based on typical Japanese fast-casual curry rice combo portions as described in published food service literature; actual restaurant portion sizes may vary. The cuisine_tags field in the input included 'Grilled Chicken Skewers' and 'Fried Chicken', which are not relevant to this dish and have been excluded from the output. The food_name has been normalised from '969. Beef Curry Rice Combo' to 'Beef Curry Rice Combo' by removing the menu item number prefix. Halal status is marked false as Japanese curry roux typically contains wheat and may contain non-halal-certified beef; consumers requiring halal certification should verify with the specific restaurant.
